narrative:
Henry Cuellar is what happens when a congressman decides that representing his constituents is less profitable than representing a foreign petrostate. The Democrat from TX-28 was indicted in May 2024 on charges of accepting approximately $600,000 in bribes from an oil and gas company owned by the government of Azerbaijan and a bank headquartered in Mexico City. The bribes were laundered through sham consulting contracts paid to shell companies owned by his wife, Imelda, who according to prosecutors "performed little or no legitimate work." In exchange, Cuellar used the "power and prestige of his office" to advance Azerbaijan's interests — delivering pro-Azerbaijan floor speeches, inserting favorable language into legislation, and lobbying colleagues on behalf of a regime that was busy waging war on neighboring Armenia.

Then, in December 2025, Donald Trump gave Cuellar a "full and unconditional" pardon — before the case even went to trial. Within days, House Democrats restored him to his seat on the Appropriations Committee, handing a man credibly accused of being a foreign agent a powerful position overseeing federal spending. The bipartisan consensus in Washington is apparently that bribery is fine as long as you are useful.

Even before the indictment, Cuellar's record read like a wish list for corporate lobbyists. He was the fourth-biggest recipient of oil and gas money in the House, pulling in over $223,000 from the industry in the 2019-2020 cycle alone. Chevron, Halliburton, Hess, and Phillips 66 all maxed out their PAC contributions. The NRA gave him an A rating and over $31,000 in donations and outside spending — making him one of only three Democrats the gun lobby bothered supporting. A dark money group that boosted his 2020 primary campaign against progressive challenger Jessica Cisneros was later revealed to be funded by — you guessed it — Big Oil.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Cuellar is a walking contradiction. Ban corporate PACs? He is powered by them. Ban dark money? It bankrolled his primary. Audit the Pentagon? He sits on Appropriations and has shown no interest. Medicare drug negotiation? He has been too busy negotiating his own consulting fees. Ban stock trading for members of Congress? Close tax loopholes? These are questions for representatives who are not under federal indictment — or, more accurately, who were not pardoned out of one.

Twenty years in Congress, and Cuellar's most notable achievement is proving that you can allegedly sell your office to a foreign government and still keep your committee assignments.

Kathy Castor is a Democrat representing Florida's 14th Congressional District, anchored in the Tampa Bay area, where she has served since 2007. A former Hillsborough County commissioner and attorney, Castor has built her congressional identity around two pillars: climate policy and healthcare. She chaired the House Select Committee on the Climate Crisis for four years and sits on the powerful Energy and Commerce Committee, where she has pushed for clean energy investment and consumer protections [1]. She is, by most measures, a reliable progressive vote — which makes the places where she goes quiet all the more interesting.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Castor's record is genuinely strong in several areas. She championed the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the first-ever Medicare drug price negotiation provisions [2], and she has pushed her Energy and Commerce Committee to expand those negotiations to cover more medications [3]. She supported the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which included police body camera requirements [4], and helped secure $382,000 for Tampa Police Department body-worn cameras [4]. She voted in favor of repealing the 2002 AUMF, issuing a statement endorsing Congress's duty to reclaim its war-making authority [5]. On campaign finance, she has expressed support for banning congressional stock trading, stating that "Americans deserve confidence that their elected leaders are working in the public interest, and not in their personal financial interest" [6].

The irony of that statement is sharpened somewhat by the fact that a government watchdog group filed a STOCK Act complaint against Castor for purchasing $45,000 in Berkshire Hathaway stock in mid-2020 and waiting a full year to disclose it [6]. The penalty for such tardiness is a possible $200 fine — a sum that would not cover the dividends on $45,000 in Berkshire Hathaway. She has also proposed legislation to ban power companies from using ratepayer revenues for lobbying and campaign donations [7], though a broader lifetime lobbying ban for former members has not appeared on her legislative agenda.

Follow the money and the picture gets more textured. In the 2023-2024 cycle, Castor's top industry contributor was health professionals at $132,366, and her campaign drew 55% of its funding from PACs [8]. AIPAC contributed $23,960, making it her top organizational donor [8]. For a member who sits on the committee that oversees healthcare and energy regulation, the steady flow of industry PAC money does not exactly scream independence from the interests she is charged with overseeing. She has taken no meaningful action on congressional term limits, banning corporate PACs, closing the carried interest loophole, or the right to repair — omissions that track neatly with the preferences of the industries keeping her campaign solvent.

Castor is the kind of Democrat who will vote the right way on the marquee bills that make it to the floor, but rarely leads on the structural reforms — banning dark money, ending gerrymandering, imposing term limits — that would make those floor votes less necessary in the first place. Her record on the 80% Bill is half full, which in Washington counts as a glass worth toasting, even if the other half is conspicuously empty.

Rep. Yvette Clarke has represented Brooklyn's 9th district since 2007, carrying the torch her Jamaican-immigrant mother lit on the New York City Council. A self-described progressive warrior, Clarke co-chairs the Medicare for All Caucus and sits on the powerful Energy and Commerce Committee, where she serves as Ranking Member of the Oversight and Investigations Subcommittee [1]. On paper, she checks many of the 80% Bill boxes. In practice, the picture is more complicated — and more interesting.

On campaign finance reform, Clarke is a lead cosponsor of the Government by the People Act, which would amplify small-dollar donations and curb big-money influence [2]. She voted for H.R. 1, the For the People Act, which bundled anti-gerrymandering provisions, the DISCLOSE Act, and voting rights protections into one sweeping package [3]. She has spoken forcefully against dark money and Citizens United, calling out "dark money groups who fund partisan gerrymandering and discriminatory ID laws" [3]. So far, so good for the Ban Dark Money, Fair Elections, and DISCLOSE Act planks of the 80% Bill.

Clarke also voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation — a centerpiece of the 80% Bill [4]. As a Congressional Progressive Caucus member, she has consistently backed taxing the ultra-wealthy and funding Social Security, voting against every Republican tax cut for the rich that has crossed her desk [5]. She cosponsored resolutions opposing the Iraq War and criticizing the PATRIOT Act early in her career, suggesting sympathy for the End Forever Wars plank [6].

But here is where the satirist sharpens the pencil. Clarke's campaign coffers tell a story her press releases prefer not to. The telecom industry — the very sector her Energy and Commerce Committee oversees — has been a reliable donor, contributing tens of thousands over multiple cycles [7]. Her 2024 fundraising leaned heavily on large individual contributions and PAC money rather than the small-dollar grassroots energy her Government by the People Act purports to champion [7]. It is the classic Washington two-step: sponsor the reform bill on Monday, cash the industry check on Tuesday.

On congressional stock trading bans, Clarke signed onto the Restoring Community Trust Act of 2026 as an original cosponsor [8]. Better late than never, though the cynic might note the issue polled at 86% approval before she climbed aboard. On term limits — another wildly popular plank — Clarke has been conspicuously silent, which is understandable for someone entering her tenth term in a safe Brooklyn seat. Asking a career politician to endorse term limits is like asking a fox to design the henhouse security system.

Clarke deserves credit for her consistent progressive voting record and genuine advocacy on voting rights and healthcare. But her reliance on industry PAC money and her selective enthusiasm for reform — loud on the popular stuff, quiet on anything that might inconvenience her personally — make her a case study in the gap between rhetoric and action that the 80% Bill exists to close.

Steve Cohen has represented Memphis's 9th District since 2007, making him one of Tennessee's longest-serving House members. Which is ironic, because when a term limits amendment came up in the Judiciary Committee, Cohen voted against it and took to the House floor to explain that his constituents keep telling him to "never retire." Nothing says man of the people quite like interpreting job security as a mandate for permanence.

To his credit, Cohen has checked a few boxes on the 80% Bill scorecard. He signed the discharge petition to ban congressional stock trading, cosponsored the STOCK Act, and voted to release the Epstein files — signing the bipartisan discharge petition to force that vote in 2025. He cosponsored the Social Security Expansion Act, which would boost benefits by $2,400 a year without raising taxes on 93 percent of Americans, and supports Medicare drug price negotiation. On paper, that is a respectable showing.

But the paper has footnotes. Cohen sits on the Judiciary and Transportation & Infrastructure committees, where he serves as Ranking Member of the Aviation Subcommittee — a perch that oversees airline policy and airport funding. The transportation and logistics industries, including aviation interests, have long been generous to Memphis-area campaigns, and Cohen has never been shy about leveraging his committee seats to direct attention toward his district's infrastructure needs. Whether that attention comes with strings attached depends on how closely you read the FEC filings.

On dark money and the DISCLOSE Act, Cohen's record is largely silent in a party that theoretically champions transparency. He has not emerged as a leading cosponsor of disclosure legislation despite sitting on the very committee with jurisdiction over campaign finance reform. For a Democrat representing the bluest district in Tennessee, the margin of safety to take bold stances is enormous — Cohen wins by 50-plus points — yet the boldness remains selective.

Cohen also has no notable record of pushing for a lobbying ban, paid family leave legislation, or corporate home-buying restrictions. The gap between what a safe-seat progressive could champion and what Cohen actually fights for is wide enough to park a FedEx cargo plane in — which, given Memphis's largest employer, might be the point.

The pattern is familiar: support the popular policies that cost nothing politically, quietly ignore the ones that might upset donor relationships, and remind everyone that seniority equals effectiveness. Cohen is not the worst offender in Congress, but in a district that hands him landslide victories, "not the worst" is a remarkably low bar for a representative with nearly two decades of tenure.

Joe Courtney has represented Connecticut's 2nd District since 2007, earning the affectionate nickname "Two Sub Joe" for his relentless advocacy to build two Virginia-class submarines per year at General Dynamics Electric Boat in Groton [1][2]. A former state legislator and lawyer, Courtney sits as Ranking Member of the House Armed Services Subcommittee on Seapower and Projection Forces -- a position that makes him one of the most consequential voices in American naval procurement and, not coincidentally, one of the defense industry's favorite Democrats [3].

On the consumer-facing planks of the 80% Bill, Courtney has a credible record. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a policy he had championed for years, arguing it was "a proven policy used for decades in the VA" [4][5]. He co-led the bipartisan Hidden Fee Disclosure Act to crack down on pharmacy benefit manager junk fees, and he supported the Lower Drug Costs Now Act to give Medicare direct negotiating power with pharmaceutical companies [6][7]. On education and labor, he has pushed for lower student loan interest rates and expanded GI Bill benefits. When asked about congressional term limits, he dismissed the idea, telling voters "we have term limits right now: voters impose them" -- a convenient position for a man seeking his ninth term [8].

But the defense spending question is where Courtney's story gets complicated, and where several 80% Bill planks -- End Forever Wars, Audit the Pentagon -- collide with his bread and butter. Over his House career, General Dynamics' PAC and employees have sent Courtney approximately $237,000, with an additional $47,000 flowing through his leadership PAC since 2009 [3][9]. Lockheed Martin and Raytheon have chipped in thousands more [9]. This is not hidden; it is the openly transactional relationship between a congressman whose district depends on submarine contracts and the companies that build them. Courtney has championed $12.4 billion Navy contracts to Electric Boat and pushed a $1 billion amendment to close a Virginia-class funding gap [10][11]. He voted to advance the FY2026 NDAA out of committee while the Pentagon continued to fail its annual audit for the sixth consecutive year [12].

Follow the money and the picture is straightforward: Courtney is a defense-industry Democrat in a defense-industry district. His top contributing sector is defense, and his legislative career has been organized around ensuring the submarines keep getting built and the shipyard workers keep getting paid. That is real constituency service -- Electric Boat employs thousands in eastern Connecticut -- but it also means Courtney has no incentive to audit the Pentagon, rein in military spending, or revisit the forever-war authorizations that justify the platforms he funds. He has never led on repealing the 2001 or 2002 AUMFs. On campaign finance reform, he benefits from the very corporate PAC system that several 80% Bill planks would dismantle.

The bottom line on Courtney: he is a reliable Democratic vote on health care, drug pricing, and labor issues, and his constituents genuinely benefit from the defense jobs he secures. But the 80% Bill asks whether representatives can serve the public interest when their campaign funding and legislative identity are built around one of the largest defense contractors on earth. Two Sub Joe has an answer to that question -- he just does not want anyone to ask it.

André Carson has represented Indiana's 7th Congressional District — essentially Indianapolis — since 2008, when he won a special election to fill the seat held by his grandmother, Julia Carson, who had passed away the previous December [1]. He is one of the few Muslims to ever serve in the United States Congress and the first Muslim to sit on the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, where he eventually became chairman of the Counterterrorism, Counterintelligence, and Counterproliferation subcommittee [2]. Before Congress, he worked as a marketing analyst for a state agency and, more formatively, as an Indiana Department of Homeland Security law enforcement officer. His district is safely blue, which means Carson has the luxury of legislative ambition without much electoral risk — a freedom he exercises selectively.

On the twenty issues that animate the 80% Bill, Carson has a genuinely strong record on several fronts. He has cosponsored the STOCK Act's successors and backed legislation to ban congressional stock trading [3]. He has supported Medicare drug price negotiation, voted for the Inflation Reduction Act's drug-pricing provisions, and cosponsored paid family leave legislation [4]. He has been a consistent advocate for police reform, including body camera mandates, which is notable for a former law enforcement officer [5]. He cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to shine light on dark money in politics and has voiced support for overturning Citizens United [6]. He has also backed efforts to audit the Pentagon and supported AUMF repeal measures to rein in forever wars — positions consistent with his intelligence committee perch, where he has seen firsthand how loosely authorized military operations can metastasize [7].

Where Carson's record gets thinner is on the economic populism front. He has not been a prominent voice on banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, closing the carried interest loophole, or pushing for a wealth tax — issues that would directly benefit his constituents in a district where median household income trails the national average [8]. He has cosponsored term limits legislation intermittently but has never made it a signature cause, which is understandable for someone who inherited a congressional seat and has held it without serious challenge for nearly two decades. His support for right-to-repair and junk fee bans has been present but largely passive, consisting of votes rather than leadership.

Follow the money and the picture fills in. Carson's top industry donors have consistently included defense and electronics manufacturers, leadership PACs, and lawyers and lobbyists [9]. In his 2024 cycle, a significant share of his funding came from PAC contributions, with defense sector PACs figuring prominently — an unsurprising arrangement for a senior Intelligence Committee member, but one that may explain his less-than-fiery posture on Pentagon accountability [10]. Real estate interests have also been steady contributors, which tracks with his silence on corporate homebuying. To his credit, Carson's overall fundraising is modest by congressional standards, and he does not appear to be building a war chest for its own sake.

The bottom line on André Carson is that he is a solidly progressive Democrat who checks most of the 80% Bill boxes but saves his loudest advocacy for the issues that do not threaten his donor relationships. He does real work on intelligence oversight and civil rights. But in a district that would reward him for swinging harder on economic populism, his restraint reads less like moderation and more like comfort — the kind of comfort that comes from a safe seat, familiar donors, and a last name that has been on Indianapolis ballots since the 1990s [11].

Bill Cassidy, the senior senator from Louisiana, is a gastroenterologist who has spent his Senate career performing exploratory procedures on popular legislation and declaring the patient unfit for surgery. A Republican who has served since 2015, Cassidy chairs the powerful Senate Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions Committee -- the first physician to hold the gavel -- which gives him enormous influence over drug pricing, paid leave, and healthcare policy [1]. He is also the man who voted to convict Donald Trump in his second impeachment trial in 2021, was promptly censured by the Louisiana Republican Party, and then carried on legislating as though nothing had happened [2]. It is the kind of move that makes you think he might be principled, until you watch what he does with his committee chairmanship.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Cassidy's record is a masterclass in appearing reasonable while blocking what voters actually want. He was named chairman of a bipartisan Senate working group on paid family leave alongside Kyrsten Sinema, generating considerable press coverage and zero legislation [3]. He talks gravely about saving Medicare and Social Security from "bankruptcy," but his proposed solutions reliably involve cutting benefits or raising the retirement age rather than, say, taxing the ultra-wealthy [4]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the first-ever Medicare drug price negotiation provisions -- and then introduced a bill to exempt small biotechs from the negotiation program, a carve-out that just happens to benefit his donors [5]. When the DISCLOSE Act came up for a vote in September 2022, Cassidy joined every other Senate Republican in blocking the dark money transparency bill [6].

The pharmaceutical industry's affection for Cassidy is not subtle. He has collected over $1 million in career contributions from pharmaceutical and health product interests, and when he became the top Republican on the HELP Committee, pharma CEOs rushed to open their checkbooks -- $5,800 from Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla, $5,000 from Eli Lilly CEO David Ricks, $2,900 from Bristol Myers Squibb CEO Giovanni Caforio [7]. In total, Cassidy received $290,375 from pharmaceutical and health products interests in the 2023-2024 cycle alone, making him one of the top GOP recipients. This is the man who now oversees Senate policy on drug pricing. The foxes have not merely entered the henhouse; they have been handed the building permits.

Cassidy's own financial disclosures reveal up to $3.5 million in historical stock trades parsed from STOCK Act filings, including positions in Toyota, Cummins, and Procter & Gamble [8]. He has not co-sponsored the Audit the Pentagon Act, has not backed the congressional stock trading ban, and has not supported the DISCLOSE Act. On term limits, banning corporate PACs, and closing tax loopholes for the wealthy, the senator from Louisiana maintains a diplomatic silence -- the kind of silence that $1 million in pharma money can buy.

The bottom line on Bill Cassidy is that he is a senator who occasionally flashes independence -- the Trump conviction vote was genuinely courageous -- but whose committee chairmanship has become a tollbooth for the industries he is supposed to regulate. He will talk about saving Social Security while blocking the revenue that would fund it, champion paid leave while producing no bill, and oversee drug pricing while cashing checks from every major pharmaceutical CEO in America. The diagnosis, doctor, is conflict of interest. The prognosis is chronic.

Judy Chu has represented the San Gabriel Valley in Congress since winning a 2009 special election, making her the first Chinese American woman elected to the U.S. House of Representatives. Now serving California's 28th district, she sits on the powerful Ways and Means Committee -- including its Health Subcommittee -- and spent 14 years as chair of the Congressional Asian Pacific American Caucus, the longest tenure in the caucus's history [1][2]. She is, by most standard Democratic scorecards, a reliable progressive vote: she backed Biden's position 100% of the time during his presidency [3]. The question, as always, is whether reliable party-line voting translates into fighting for the structural reforms that 80% of Americans actually want.

On the policy substance of the 80% Bill, Chu has a genuinely strong record in several areas. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a landmark she championed from her perch on Ways and Means [4]. She co-led efforts to pass universal paid family leave as part of the Build Back Better Act [5]. She is an original cosponsor of the Social Security 2100 Act to expand and fund Social Security benefits [6]. She introduced the End Tax Breaks for Dark Money Act with Senator Sheldon Whitehouse, targeting the loophole that lets billionaires dodge capital gains taxes on donations to dark money groups [7]. She cosponsored H.R. 1, the For the People Act, which addressed gerrymandering, campaign finance, and voting rights [8]. She has stated support for eliminating gerrymandering and strengthening ethics rules [9]. On the reform ledger, she has shown up where it counts on drug pricing, paid leave, Social Security, and dark money.

Where the narrative gets more interesting is on congressional stock trading. Chu was among the 97 members of Congress flagged by The New York Times for financial trades that raised conflict-of-interest questions [10]. In 2019, her husband purchased between $45,000 and $150,000 in shares of ApolloMed, a healthcare company headquartered in her district whose patients rely heavily on Medicare -- the very program her Health Subcommittee oversees [11]. The company's fortunes are directly tied to the policy decisions flowing through her committee. Watchdog groups from the Campaign Legal Center to Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington flagged the trade as the kind of inherent conflict that makes the public distrust Congress [10]. Despite this, Chu has not been a prominent cosponsor of the leading stock trading ban bills that have come through the House, a notable silence from someone whose own family portfolio illustrates precisely why such a ban is needed.

Follow the money more broadly, and the picture stays complicated. Chu serves on Ways and Means, the committee that writes the tax code and oversees healthcare policy -- two areas where corporate lobbying dollars flow like water. Her career fundraising through OpenSecrets shows the usual constellation of PAC contributions and individual donors from industries her committee regulates [12]. She led 25 House members in demanding adequate IRS funding to pursue wealthy tax cheats [13], and she voted against Republican tax bills she called "handouts to the wealthiest Americans" [14]. The progressive tax rhetoric is real, but the ApolloMed situation suggests that when the conflict of interest lands in her own portfolio, the urgency for reform dims.

The bottom line on Judy Chu is a familiar Democratic paradox: strong on many of the 80% Bill's planks -- drug pricing, paid leave, Social Security, dark money -- but conspicuously quiet on banning the very stock trading that her own family's finances made controversial. She has been in Congress since 2009. That is a long time to fight for reform and still not cosponsor a stock trading ban. Voters in the 28th district should ask whether their representative's silence on that one issue is principled or personal.

Eric A. “Rick” Crawford, Republican of Arkansas’s 1st congressional district, has represented the rural eastern half of the state since 2011 — making him the first Republican to hold the seat since Reconstruction[1]. A former Army bomb disposal technician turned rodeo cowboy turned radio farm reporter, Crawford parlayed his agricultural background into a seat on the House Agriculture Committee, where he has shaped two Farm Bills and chaired multiple subcommittees[2]. In January 2025, Speaker Mike Johnson elevated him to chairman of the House Intelligence Committee after removing Rep. Mike Turner, who had committed the unforgivable sin of questioning Donald Trump[3]. Crawford now oversees both agricultural policy and the nation’s intelligence apparatus, a combination that would seem unlikely anywhere but Washington.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Crawford’s record is a patchwork of occasional alignment and predictable opposition. He voted with the near-unanimous House majority to release the Epstein files in November 2025, joining the 427-1 landslide[4]. He has introduced the PASS Act with Elise Stefanik to restrict foreign adversaries from acquiring American agricultural land, putting him squarely in line with the Protect US Farmland article[5]. But Crawford voted with every other House Republican against the Inflation Reduction Act in 2022, which included the Medicare drug price negotiation provisions that 80% of Americans support[6]. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, impose term limits, audit the Pentagon, end dark money, or create paid family leave — the bread and butter of the 80% agenda.

Crawford voted for the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, claiming it would help “middle-income families in my district”[7], though independent analyses showed the lion’s share of benefits accruing to corporations and the wealthy. He has shown no appetite for closing the carried interest loophole, taxing the ultra-wealthy, or funding Social Security’s long-term solvency. On campaign finance reform, Crawford has been silent — no cosponsorship of the DISCLOSE Act, no support for overturning Citizens United, no moves to ban corporate PACs.

Crawford’s top donor industries track neatly with his committee assignments. Agriculture, defense, and steel production feature prominently in his campaign coffers, with steel production ranking as his number-two industry donor in the 2024 cycle[8]. As a senior Agriculture Committee member and now Intelligence chairman, Crawford sits at the intersection of two powerful funding streams — agribusiness PACs that need favorable farm policy and defense interests that need friendly oversight. The crop insurance industry, which receives billions in federal subsidies that Crawford helps authorize, has been a reliable contributor throughout his career[9].

The bottom line on Crawford is familiar: a veteran Republican who will occasionally land on the popular side of a 95-5 vote like the Epstein files, but who reliably opposes the structural reforms — on taxes, campaign finance, and corporate power — that define the 80% Bill. He represents a district where Donald Trump won by 40 points, and he legislates accordingly[10].

Chris Coons has occupied Joe Biden's old Senate seat since 2010, and he has spent his tenure cultivating a reputation as the Senate's most affable centrist -- the guy who will reach across the aisle, find the bipartisan compromise, and make sure nobody important gets too upset in the process [1]. A former corporate counsel at W.L. Gore & Associates and a Yale-educated lawyer, Coons arrived in Washington with the institutional DNA of Delaware already encoded: a state where corporations outnumber people and the credit card industry writes its own rules [2]. He serves on the Appropriations Committee, including as top Democrat on the Defense Subcommittee, giving him a front-row seat to the Pentagon spending he has shown little appetite to question [3].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Coons presents an instructive case study in the gap between rhetoric and action. He has been a vocal champion of the DISCLOSE Act, eloquently denouncing the "flood of dark money" unleashed by Citizens United and calling for transparency in campaign spending [4][5]. He co-introduced legislation to repeal the 1991 and 2002 AUMFs, correctly noting that "Congress must reaffirm its constitutional authority over war powers" [6]. He voted for the STOCK Act's disclosure requirements [7]. These are real positions on real bills. But on the structural reforms that would actually redistribute power -- a wealth tax, congressional term limits, a lifetime lobbying ban, banning corporate PACs -- Coons has been somewhere between silent and opposed. He notably declined to support Elizabeth Warren's Ultra-Millionaire Tax Act and has not embraced Medicare for All or Bernie Sanders' tuition proposals, instead offering "centrist plans for similar issues" that consistently arrive with fewer teeth [8].

The pharmaceutical industry tells you everything you need to know about where Coons' centrism actually centers. Pharmaceuticals and health products are his number one career industry donor at $225,713, followed by securities and investment at $179,300, real estate at $170,000, commercial banks at $133,000, and insurance at $115,500 [9]. In 2020 alone, he was the third-highest recipient of pharmaceutical industry donations in Congress, pulling in over $124,000 from the likes of AstraZeneca, Merck, Pfizer, and Johnson & Johnson [10]. He supported the America Invents Act, which strengthened Big Pharma's intellectual property monopolies, and opposed importing cheaper drugs from Canada [10]. When COVID hit and Mitch McConnell demanded corporate immunity from lawsuits as a condition of stimulus relief, Coons broke with his own party to support the idea -- a move that happened to benefit his second-largest donor, Apollo Global Management, whose executives had given him $79,100 [11]. Apollo's three billionaire co-founders -- Marc Rowan, Leon Black, and Joshua Harris -- all wrote personal checks [11].

Coons has taken some genuinely good positions: supporting the DISCLOSE Act, voting to repeal outdated war authorizations, and pushing for PBM transparency. He backed paid family leave in principle and has not actively undermined Social Security. But the pattern is unmistakable: when a popular reform threatens the industries that fund his campaigns -- pharmaceutical price negotiation, corporate PAC bans, the carried interest loophole, taxing the ultra-wealthy -- Coons finds a reason to be the reasonable moderate who just cannot quite get there. His progressive challenger Jess Scarane put it plainly when she blasted him for prioritizing corporate donors over constituents [12]. Delaware may be a small state, but its senior senator has mastered the art of sounding populist while governing for the corporate class that calls Wilmington home.
